sentences.text: Well, when I came to this country, I felt that people didn't know much about what I lived through, what all the survivors lived through.
And when I was invited somewhere, I was trying to cover up my tattoo.
I was introduced to Max Factor stick makeup, but this didn't work.
So when I went to a formal college ball, they gave me long gloves.
And I never took my left glove off.
I never took my gloves off, period.
For some reason, I was ashamed of my tattoo.
I was ashamed of what I went through.
I saw a teenagers that lived such a normal, progressive life, and how much I lost.
I never went through a teenage life.
I never went through the moods that-- that the teenagers go through now, that I look at and I said, where was I?
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Yes, I-- I did think of removing it.
It would have have to be, I guess, a plastic surgery, which I would never do.
And actually, now I just was gonna live as long as I can with it, and die.
And that's my identification from my childhood.
You know, I was sent with the group.
You know, they count you out, and these groups go here and these groups go there.
By the way, my aunt was with me, counted out.
We were trying to be close to each other.
We were-- supposedly we went-- we lived in Birkenau in those barracks.
But we used to go to work to the Auschwitz.
There were brick buildings.
And we use to fold and sort out linens.

They were-- the linens were coming-- I think they were confiscated and taken away from the people that were coming from France, and Hungary, and Romania, and Czechoslovakia-- from all the Jewish people that were told that they were going to work.
But they wound up in Auschwitz, and they were cremated.
And the clothes-- the clothes and the possessions were confiscated.
I personally didn't.
It could be that some people did find.
I-- I didn't.
We had to work quietly, have our ration at noon.
I remember when we were issued-- because this was like a privileged job.
They were selecting the youngest, and the strongest, and the prettiest, I guess, to do-- to do the work.
Once in awhile, they allow us to take, or they brought, like, a few sweaters that were handmade.
And we used to undo them.
Excuse me, my stomach is growling.
[LAUGHS] It's-- it's-- we used to undo the sweaters.
And a couple of the girls, I don't know how they got needles, knitting needles.
And they used to knit, I remember, turtlenecks for the group, in this room.
We were about five of us in this particular room.
So we all wound up with turtleneck sweaters.
And we all helped each other.
And when we used to go back to the camp, the lights were out quite early.
And I used to hear in my barrack the screaming and the shouting from the experimental barracks, which were not too far from me.
And those were the people-- usually they were twins-- that they used to do different experiments on them.
At that time, I didn't know what experiments were done.
But the screaming was horrible.
Later on, we found out that they were experimenting-- cauterizing them and-- and experimenting, putting them in a ice water and hot water-- whatever it was done.
I don't remember.
I have no recollection.
As a matter of fact, I went three years ago to Auschwitz, and there was only one barrack standing with lots of chimneys in Birkenau.
But the-- the guide took us first to Auschwitz, and I said to my husband, you know, I don't remember the brick buildings.
I remember sleeping in a barracks.
And then he took us.
Like, five minutes later, he says, you were in Birkenau.
And sure enough, I showed my husband how I lived-- long, like a brick stove in the center; and the bunks that you had to slide into them.
You couldn't stand up or even sit up-- and how many of us used to live and sleep in those bunkers.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --yes, I remember when they evacuated us from Auschwitz.
And that was the death march.
And in this death march, my aunt was shot.
She had a little heart problem.
She couldn't walk as fast in the snow and the freezing weather as they wanted us.
And I was marching with her.
And she says, let me just sit down and rest a little bit.
And I said, Aunt, you know you can't do that.
Walk with me.
I will help you.
And the women around wanted to help her, but she was too exhausted.
She says, no, you go head.
I will catch up with you.
And as I walked away from her, I was afraid to turn around, but I heard a shot.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --the people that caught up to me, that saw what happened, told me that she was shot.
She was a young woman in her late 20s.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] She had two children that were also killed.
And I remember the march.
It was just before we heard the rumors, and actually, the air raids, before the Russians were coming in.
And we slept in-- on the farms in the-- where you keep animals.
How do you call this?
The barn.
In the barns.
And a lot of people were able to escape during this time.
I was alone.
I didn't know where I would go, what I would do.
So I just stayed with the group.
And then we were loaded into those cattle trains.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember that my two toes on my left foot were frozen.
And when I was unloaded from this train, I couldn't move my jaw.
Because the trains were open.
It was snowing.
It was freezing.
We didn't eat or drink for a couple days.
And they put us on the side of the rails.
Because the army transport, I guess, they had the priorities.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember I arrived to a little-- I don't know if it was a camp.
At that point, I was sick.
And I also contact typhus.
And you know, you couldn't admit that you were ill.
So I had temperature.
And I remember on the train, there was a woman doctor.
I don't remember her name or who she was, and I would never recognize her again.
She did not speak my native language or German.
She spoke Hungarian.
She gave me some kind of a ointment on my foot and my toes, because I had a gangrene.
And when we arrived to this-- like I said, I don't know if it was a camp, but I remember it was a factory-- ammunition factory.
And we were stationed in-- in the barracks.
She was still with me.
And you couldn't get hot water or cold water to soak your feet.
But the ointment that she put, and some kind of a gauze on my two toes-- when we arrived to those barracks, she took it off.
And the part of the gangrene came off.
And she says, you will heal.
She bandaged me again.
And yes, I did heal.
And I don't know who the woman was.
And I guess I survived the typhus.
People around me, somehow they got water.
And from that, came-- I was shipped with the same group-- we weren't there very long-- to Bergen-Belsen.
In Bergen-Belsen, I met one of my mother's friends.
And I remember this lady to be beautiful.
And she was on the heavy side.
But when I saw her, she was very, very skinny.
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
But I remember going, again, through the segregation.
And from there, after being there maybe a couple weeks in Bergen-Belsen, I don't know how long, I was shipped to Mauthausen--
--Austria.
No.

Most of the time I remember there was stress-- sitting and waiting-- I don't know for what; being taken out to be counted, and counted again; and seeing lots of people looking like skeletons, shaved heads, just walking, like in a daze.
I guess, I, myself, must have been in a daze, because I don't remember much.
[SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Hoping that I will live.
I-- I remember being very hungry, thinking about food.
I remember as a child, I never liked potatoes.
If my mother serve us mashed potatoes on the table, I would not eat them.
And I remembered that when I was very hungry.
And I promised myself that when I live, I will never say no to potatoes.
And today, potatoes are my best vegetable.
I can have potatoes in the morning, at night, at noon.
I love potatoes.
You thought of survival.
You thought of food.
You thought how to escape the whips of the SS; how not to be bitten by those vicious dogs; how to stay in the line correctly and obey.
You saw a lot of people dying in front of you.
You tried to block many things out.
At least, I tried.
And, um--
I was trying to block out what was going on around me, what was happening.
It was there, but I always knew and hoped that I will live and be reunited with my parents.
I was always hoping.
I just knew that I will live.

And you know, the people that were giving me their extra ration, older people than I was-- I was listening to their stories, their education, the musics, the operas that they used to go to, the schoolings that they went through.
There were a lot of educated people; people that had different experiences; people that lost husbands, and children, and parents, and homes.
There were people from different countries that you were thrown together.
For instance, I never knew that lettuce or cabbage could be eaten raw.
I-- I never had this at home.
And once in awhile, some of the women used to bring-- they worked in a kitchen, or they were, for some reason, given a gift-- a raw cabbage or a lettuce.
They used to share.
We were very hungry at that point.
You would-- I ate potato peel.
Today, we know that potatoes are very healthy and things that are raw and unpeeled, but at that time, we didn't know that.
But we ate them unwashed-- if you could find any scrap to feed yourself, to keep up your strength.
To do this work, yes.
I have no idea.
I was-- I think it was my luck that I was chosen to be among this group.
But they-- everybody in this group that done this work seemed to be much younger, capable, you know.
If you had a blemish on your body, they used to send you to crematorium.
No.
No, not to my knowledge.
No.
But when we went back to the barracks, and the lights needed to be turned off, I was exposed the first time-- not personally, but I was explained what happening.
I've seen two women being in love in the same bunk not too far from my bunk.
And I couldn't understand this.
It was hard for me.
My aunt explained to me that they were two women that love each other.
And, um--
Lesbians, yes.
And I guess it must have happened.
They were from Greece.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
They were Jewish women.
Not that I remember.
We were only Jewish women in our camp.
Now, there were other camps that there were other Poles.
But we did not get together.
They kept us separate.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know, not really.

I don't know if I'm a late bloomer, and at that time it didn't even occur to me; or I didn't think of it; or maybe it's because I was lucky and I wasn't approach; or because I was shield and sheltered during our marches.
Only during our counting did I have to stay erect in being counted.
But when we were marching, the women sort of were trying to cover me up front.
And when I passed the gates, they were covering me sort of in the back with their own body.
They were crowding closer to each other.
No.
No.
As a matter of fact, I'm a late bloomer in this respect, too.
As a matter of fact, when I got married, I thought that I will never be able to bear children.
And I went to a doctor, this gynecologist in Germany.
And they said that because what I lived through and the malnutrition, that I will start my menstrual periods later.
I did, but I was never regular.
And I received a very deep therapy, radiation therapy, after I was married.
It's banned since then, because in the medical field it's proven that it's too dangerous.
I guess to awaken in my system whatever was asleep, or to stimulate, I should say-- to be able to bear children.
And I guess it worked, because I got pregnant right after.
Unfortunately, I miscarried.
And yes, I had-- I have three children.
And I had six pregnancies, so I miscarried three.
But--
OK.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mauthausen was-- I remember when we came in-- had a tremendous gate.
And next camp to us that was separated by barbed wires, they had men in this camp.
So through the barbed wires, you could communicate.
And somebody told me that I have two uncles in that camp by name Immergluck.
Now in this camp, where the men were, there were both Jews and other religions and nationalities.
Well, somehow my uncles-- they were my father's brothers-- found out that another Immergluck came with a transport.
They thought that they were one-- some-- of their wives.
They didn't expect me to be alive.
And sure enough, we communicated through the wires now.
Barbed wire-- it wasn't just one barbed wire.
There was like a tremendous alley between, and then barbed wires on either side.
Dolec Immergluck and Marion Immergluck.
It was my father's second youngest and a younger brother.
They told me that my grandfather was cremated in Mauthausen-- that a transport came and they cremated him there.
They couldn't do nothing about it.
I didn't even know that Mauthausen had a crematorium.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I also witnessed in this transport that I came from Bergen-Belsen to Mauthausen, a woman giving birth to a premature child.
I don't know how she got pregnant.
I don't know when she was-- came into the camp.
Because there were different years that people start, unfortunately, being prisoners.
They called us prisoners.
And I always wonder, what did I do wrong other than being born to a Jewish parents and being Jewish?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So I witnessed this as a very young girl.
And then one day, we heard that the war was over.
I saw her giving-- being in labor, other women being with her.
I remember she could not scream very loud.
And I just remember her being in a position, and the baby coming out.
The baby was wrapped, and I don't know.
I think the baby survived, because we were liberated very shortly after.
